## [00:00 - 03:10]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about one of the key decisions you have to make for your webinar, which is the who, meaning who's the group that is most likely to buy your product? Because that's the group you want to attend your webinar. That's your target market. That's your avatar, the group of people that you are looking to serve. So I find this happening all the time. I'll be talking to people and I'll say, so who's the target market for your product? And they'll give me what I call the kiss of death answer, which is, oh, this product is perfect for everyone. Whenever I hear that, I have serious doubts about the product. Because whenever you target everyone, you target no one and people can tell and it's not right for them. So let's talk about how to identify your target market. So there is a concept in marketing. We call your target avatar. So an avatar is basically a description of the person, the potential buyer that you're trying to reach. And what you're trying to do here is you're trying to describe them in a way that is incredibly useful without getting into a level of detail that becomes not useful. Okay. So the question that I ask is this, based on the research you've done. Who is the type of person who is most likely to want the transformation that your product offers? And when you do this, I recommend that you get quite specific. So let me give you an example of this. Previously, you have seen some things about female entrepreneurs and how to do market research to discover the needs of female entrepreneurs. Well, let's continue down that path because you know what? There's lots of different types of female entrepreneurs. There's lots of different ways that they're things that they're dealing with in their lives. There's lots of different ways that they're going to market. There's lots of different ways that they look at their business. And so what I'm recommending is that you don't just target female entrepreneurs, but that you get specific and target a certain subgroup of that marketplace. So let's just imagine for a moment that we are going to target a very specific group, female entrepreneurs with kids still living at home.

## [03:12 - 06:13]

Now, as soon as I do that, what I just did is I just boxed out a whole bunch of other people because that's what marketing is. Marketing is the process of deciding who you want not to buy your product so that you can focus in on the group of people who is most likely to want to buy your product. So I'm not dealing with people who are empty nesters. I'm not dealing with people who are perhaps wanting to get a bunch of venture capital money. And so they're working 1820 hours a day with the staff of 5200 people and all those things. I'm looking very specifically at a very tight group here, female entrepreneurs with kids still living at home. And so this particular group is having to balance motherhood with family life. And so they're stretched beyond breaking and constantly feeling guilt at having to choose between two or more very good things. So every time she decides to work on her business, she's deciding to ignore the children and put them in front of the TV or get a nanny for them or something like that. And both of those are important to her. And so she's having to make that choice and she feels guilty no matter how she makes that choice. And consequently, she's constantly feeling like a failure. Not only because she's having to make these choices and she sees the disappointed look in her kids eyes, or she sees that her product isn't performing as well as it should. And she thinks, if only I could spend a little bit more time doing that, but she's constantly wrestling with the two. By the way, I bet you know some people who fit this category and you can see exactly what I'm talking about. And one of the things that happens with this particular target group is that they tend to feel like a failure as she sees her peers making choices that bring them success that she feels she can't achieve because of her choices. So they may be making choices to put their kids in preschool the age of two, and so they can spend a solid day, or they may be deciding to focus on their children and put the business just as a side project for a while. But no matter what she's doing, she's constantly feeling guilty because she's comparing herself to people who are succeeding in the areas where she simply can't because she has to balance both of them. Can you see how this is so much more powerful to target this female entrepreneur than generically female entrepreneurs?

## [06:13 - 09:16]

Because I can talk to this female entrepreneur. Now, it'd be even better if I was one, but just imagine that you're a female who's been through all this. And so now you're talking to them. They immediately identified with this much more powerfully than if you just came in and said, let's talk to you female entrepreneurs about how to build your business. And we know that you've got some challenges in your life. And it's like so much more mushy. But I get that specificity in there. Now I can emotionally deal with the problems that she's got both in the marketing course and in the course itself and the product that I'm selling because she knows that I'm understanding her and serving her specific needs. Now, as I talk about specificity, I do caution you to be careful with creating too much specificity. I see this one happen all the time. Also, I'll have somebody walk in with a with a target avatar and they got a picture of her and and they'll say she's she's 28 through 32. She has dyed blonde hair. She drives a used car with 100,000 miles on it. She's eating mac and cheese and hot dogs for dinner because that's what she can do in 10 minutes that her kids will eat. She's 15 pounds overweight from eating that mac and cheese. And she has a partner who's working two jobs just to support her dreams. Now, all of these things can be valuable insights to help you maybe describe your avatar situation, but be careful not to use those specifics to exclude people from your marketplace. Because that female entrepreneur who has kids still at home and who's trying to balance their business and their kids doesn't have to fit all those specifics. I just showed you the 28 through 32 and etc. To be a potential customers of yours. They could be a vegan instead of eating mac and cheese and hot dogs. She may drive a nice car because she needs to entertain clients. She may be older or younger. She may even be a natural redhead. And so realize that if you get too specific, you actually start pushing people away that should be in your target audience. So a target, an ideal target market is made up of people who self identify themselves as being part of that market. Now, as I say this, I realize that we do have a potential problem in the example that I've used in this video. And that is. Well, let me just step out one step further entrepreneurs. So people. Recognize their entrepreneurs. But if you go up to somebody and say what do you do for a living? Most of the entrepreneurs will not say I'm an entrepreneur.

## [09:16 - 12:33]

They will say I'm a plumber. I'm a coach. I'm a speaker. I help people do this. In other words, they're not self identifying themselves as an entrepreneur. They realize that they are one. But they self identify themselves as what it is that they do. Not the business type. Now it is slightly different for female entrepreneurs because there's been a there's a lot of business done helping female entrepreneurs be able to succeed. And so it's a little bit more specific, but realize that you need to identify a group who really if you say to them. This is for plumbers. The plumbers immediately get it. The electricians say that's not for me. So you need a phrase that when you say it, people go, that's me. They immediately know what they belong in that group. Secondly, an ideal target market. And the reason why I'm saying this is because this is your homework here is to identify your ideal target market avatar. So secondly, an ideal target market is big enough to justify the effort and expense to market to them. There are some businesses where there are a grand total of 500 people in the entire world who do that. Okay, now you may be able to do very, very well marketing to those 500 people in the world. Or it simply may not be big enough to justify the expense and the effort to market to them. Personally, I prefer bigger chunks of people. I prefer groups of five to 10 million people in the target market, which enables me to be able to run ads over and over again over time and constantly have new people that those ads can reach. While we're on the subject of ads, an ideal target market can be targeted with advertising. So if you have something that is identifiable, but you can't reach them with advertising, that's going to be tough. It's a lot easier if you've got something that can be specifically targeted via advertising, which will enable you to be able to expand that targeting out to things like Google and Instagram. And Pinterest and other places because you've got a bigger target that you're able to approach. So I invite you as you work on your target market avatar to think through these three elements of a target ideal target market to get specific. Try to find ways that you can talk to that person in a way that emotionally reaches his or her strong points and weak points and challenges and opportunities in their lives so that you can become the person that they turn to for that solution.
